Dear Mr. Cohan: 
Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Santa 
Barbara County Planning Commissioners Margaret S. Hamister, 
Donald E. Lahr and Kenneth R. Stillman. 
FACTS 
The facts, as I understand them, are that Santa Barbara 
County is currently reviewing and revising its zoning 
ordinance. 
This review includes that portion of the zoning 
ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities 
(Divisions 8 and 10). 
The County's Resource Management 
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of 
Divisons 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. 
This 
draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were 
conducted. 
As a result of these hearings, and after discussions 
by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of 
Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing 
textual changes requested by the Commission. 
The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to 
approve the Revised Hearing Draft. 
If passed, the Draft will be 
submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, 
modified or disapproved. 
(See Government Code Sections 
65854-65857)!/ 
However, the recommendations of the Planning 
!/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982 
Page 2 
Commission will greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in 
its final decision regarding the oil and gas regulations. 
The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter 
the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit 
procedures. 
These changes include: 
1. 
Banning oil development in seven areas where 
development was previously allowed; 
2. 
Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas 
exploration and production drilling by eliminating a 
requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a 
permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use 
Permit be obtained; 
3. 
Establishing more stringent "development 
standards"~1 that must be met for exploratory oil and gas 
drilling; onshore oil and gas production; treatment and 
processing facilities; refining; marine terminals; and oil 
and gas pipelines; 
4. 
Establishing a procedure whereby a ministerially 
granted exploration and production permit may be obtained if 
a project meets certain, established criteria,ll but if 
the criteria are not met, a discretionary permit must be 
sought from the Planning Commission. 
This new procedure 
provides the Planning Commission with more discretion'than 
under the existing system.il 
~I For example, in the Revised Hearing Draft, the 
"development standards" for treatment and processing facilities 
require noise and noxious odor limitations, a fence surrounding 
the facility and provisions to prevent erosion and flood damage. 
11 
These criteria establish that the project will not 
have a significant environmental impact. 
il Under the existing system, a dicretionary, rather 
than ministerial, permit must be obtained if drilling will be 
within 1,000 feet of a zone on which drilling is not allowed. 
The new system requires a discretionary permit if drilling is 
within this 1,000 foot setback, as well as in many other 
situations, such as where the company plans to build a treatment 
or processing facility. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982 
Page 3 
5. 
Allowing oil companies additional flexibility in 
obtaining permits for exploratory wells. 
Currently, one 
permit is issued for both the exploration and production 
stages of oil and gas development. 
In the case of 
unproductive wells,21 this results in unnecessary, lengthy 
environmental review of the production phase. 
The proposed 
procedures allow oil companies to first file for an 
exploration permit and later file for a production permit, 
if the well is successful. 
6. 
Replacing existing, vague provisions for oil and 
gas pipelines with provisions requiring the Planning 
Commission to issue discretionary permits. 
"Development 
standards" are also established for pipelines (see #3, 
above) • 
Your question is whether Commissioners Hamister, Lahr and 
Stillman may vote on the Revised Hearing Draft, or whether they 
have conflicts of interests requiring them to abstain. 
PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER 
Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock 
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000. 
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall 
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision 
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. 
(Section 
87103(a) ). 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making 
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect 
Exxon Corporation. 
In voting to approve or reject the final draft of the 
proposed changes to the zoning ordinance, Commissioner Hamister 
will be making and participating in the making of a governmental 
21 
Most of the exploratory drilling results in 
unproductive wells. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982 
Page 4 
decision.~1 It is true that the Board of Supervisors makes 
the final decision to change the oil and gas regulations, 
however, the law specifies that the Planning Commission has a 
major role in any zoning ordinance change. 
The Commission is 
required to hold hearings and to make a written recommendation 
(the Final Revised Hearing Draft) to the Board of Supervisors 
and, if the Board deviates from the recommendations, the Board 
must ask for an additional report and recommendation from the 
Planning Commission (Sections 65854-65857). 
Thus, the content 
of the Final Revised Hearing Draft will substantially influence 
the final changes that will be made to the oil and gas 
regulations. 
A decision to approve or reject the draft of the proposed 
zoning changes will have a material financial effect on a 
company the size of Exxon Corporation, if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the proposed changes would increase or decrease 
Exxon's annualized gross income by $100,000, its annual net 
income by $50,000, or the value of its assets or liabilities by 
$100,000. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1) (A)-(C) .)21 
In determining whether such a financial effect i~ foreseeable, 
certainty is not required and there must only be a "substantial 
likelihood" 
that the effect will occur. 
(Thorner Opinion, No. 
75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, copy enclosed.) 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed changes will 
have a material financial effect on Exxon Corporation, if the

whether the effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease:

company plans to continue the exploration, drilling and

will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on

A decision will have a material financial effect on a

foreseeable that the decision will increase or decrease the

The gross income of Lahr Motors, in 1981, was

Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, if adopted, will increase or 
decrease the company's gross revenues by $6,000, or meet any of

disqualify himself. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed zoning 
changes will restrict oil and gas drilling and, therefore, cause 
a potential decline in the company's motor sales. 
However, Lahr 
Motors sells motors to businesses other than oil companies and 
it performs repairs, neither of which will be affected by the 
proposed zoning changes. 
Commissioner Lahr must, in good faith, 
determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed 
changes, if implemented, will affect his business to the extent 
described in the percentage tests. 
He must disqualify himself 
from the vote, if necessary. 
Because Commissioner Lahr owns 86.1% of Lahr Motors, income

(Section 82030). 
Therefore, Commissioner Lahr is also

any clients of Lahr Motors who provided the company with

on the Revised Hearing Draft,ll/ that Commission Lahr's pro

If it is reasonably foreseeable that

COMMISSIONER STILLMAN 
Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the 
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over

Lompoc 
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa

The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are 
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling 
on the land. 16/ 
Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the 
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them. 
Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in 
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director 
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the

If the amount paid during the 12 month period 
was $291, or more, then Commissioner Lahr must disqualify

15/ 
The facts regarding Lompoc Oil are drawn from your 
letters; my conversations with your office; a letter from 
David A. Anderson, attorney for Get Oil Out, Inc., and GOO Legal

proposed zoning and permit changes, if the changes, if adopted, 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
Lompoc Oil. 
(Sections 87100 and 87103(a) and (d).) 
A decision 
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of 
Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income and $5,000 in net 
income for 1981), if the decision will affect the company by 
$1,000 or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b) (1) (A)-(C).) 
The mineral rights owned by Lompoc Oil are on land zoned 
Agricultural - 100 (meaning that there is a 100 acre minimum) . 
Under the exisiting zoning ordinance, drilling is allowed on 
this type of land as a matter of right, unless the drilling is 
within 1,000 feet of another zone that does not allow drilling. 
In the latter case, a Conditional Use Permit must be obtained, 
which involves a discretionary review by the Planning 
Commmission. 
Under the proposed zoning changes, the 700 acres 
will probably be designated as Agricultural II land. 
On 
Agricultural II land, drilling is allowed as a matter of right, 
if the land meets certain criteria. 
If the criteria are not 
met, an Exploratory Plan must be prepared and the Planning 
Commission's discretionary approval must be obtained. 
Based on these facts, it is not reasonably foreseeable that 
the proposed changes will have a material financial effect on 
Lompoc Oil because there will be little or no impact on the 
company's gross income, net income, or assets. 
The changes may, 
ultimately, cost Lompoc Oil some additonal money in permit 
costs, however, the company currently has no plans to develop 
the 200 acres and the remaining 550 acres will be developed by 
Santa Fe Energy Company. 
Also, any increase in the Planning 
Commission's discretion in granting or denying permits is 
unlikely to affect the company because, assuming that a 
discretionary permit is necessary at all, the zoning of the land 
makes the approval of a permit highly probable. 17/ 
Thus, 
Commissioner Stillman may vote on the proposed changes, as he 
does not appear to have a conflict. 
CONCLUSION 
If Exxon Corporation plans to continue to explore, drill and 
develop oil and gas facilities in Santa Barbara County, then it

is reasonably foreseeable that the company will be materially

It appears likely that the proposed 
changes, if adopted, will either materially affect clients of 
his business who are sources of income to him, or the business 
itself. 
In either case, disqualification is necessary. 
Lastly, 
Commissioner Stillman does not have a conflict of interest and 
may vote on the proposed changes. 
I would like to point out that the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act only allow this office to render advice as 
to whether a future action of a public official will constitute 
a violation of the Act. 
Consequently, I cannot provide you with 
advice on whether Commissioners Hamister and Lahr 181 violated 
the Act, in the past, by making or participating in the making 
of decisions involving changes to the oil and gas regulations. 
In addition, the staff's advice provides the Commissioners with 
immunity from enforcement actionsl11 only if the advice is

18329) •

Even 
if the past activities were in violation of the Act, the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not invalidate these 
decisions. 
However, under special circumstances, these 
decisions may be set aside in a lawsuit brought by a person 
residing in the jurisdiction. 
(Section 91003(b}.)

Santa Barbara County Planning Commission 
We request that the Commission advise us as to whether the 
following three Planning Commissioners may participate in the 
hearings and decision of the Planning Commission on revisions 
to the County's Planning and Zoning Ordinance as they pertain 
to oil and gas energy development. 
The Planning Commission is revising the Planning and Zoning 
Ordinance pursuant to its authority under Gov. Code § 65853 
et seq. 
Especially see § 65855. 
As part of the proposed 
Zoning Ordinance Divisions 8 and 10 deal with matters 
affecting the oil and gas industry. 
A summary of Division 8 
is attached as Exhibit A. 
Attached as Exhibits Band C 
respectively are copies of Division 8 "Energy Facilities," 
in its original and in its most recently revised forms. 
Attached as Exhibit D is a copy of Division 10, "Permit 
Procedures." 
Our request for this advice arises from a citizen's complaint 
of a possible conflict as to Commissioners Lahr, Hamister and 
Stillman. 
Copies of their disclosure statements are attached 
as Exhibits E, F and G. 
Some additional factual data may be helpful.

This review includes that portion of the

The County's Resource Management 
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of 
Divisions 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. 
This draft was presented to the Planning Commission, who held 
hearings, requsted textual changes, and ultimately developed the 
Final Revised Hearing Draft. 
The changes proposed by the 
Planning Commission would substantially alter the existing oil

PLANNING COMMISSIONER HAMISTER 
Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock 
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000. 
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall 
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision 
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. 
(Section 
87103(a)). 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making 
and participating in any decisions which will materially affect 
Exxon Corporation. 
Under Commission regulations, a decision 
will have a material financial effect on a company the size of 
Exxon, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the 
decision will be to increase or decrease Exxon's annualized 
gross income by $100,000, its annual net income by $50,000, or 
the value of its assets or liabilities by $100,000. 
(2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1) (A)-(C).) 
As I discussed in my advice letter to Mr. Cohan, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to amend the oil and 
gas regulations is a decision will have a material financial 
effect on Exxon Corporation. 
(See my letter for the analysis.) 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister is and was prohibited from making, 
or participating in the making of, this decision. 
In my advice letter, I confined my analysis to whether it 
was proper for Commissioner Hamister to vote on the future, 
final decision to approve or reject the Final Revised Draft of 
the changes to the oil and gas regulations. 
However, 
Commissioner Hamister may have already participated in 
discussions or decisions occurring prior to this final vote. 
In 
my letter, I did not discuss the propriety of her participation 
and any enforcement action would, of course, focus on these past 
activities.

described in the percentage tests.

COMMISSIONER STILLMAN 
Commissioner Stillman owns a little over 1 percent of the 
stock issued by Lompoc Oil Developing Company, worth over 
$1,000, and he sits on the company's Board of Directors. 
Lompoc 
Oil owns the mineral rights to approximately 750 acres in Santa 
Barbara County. 
The mineral rights on 550 of the acres are 
leased to Santa Fe Energy Company, which has not begun drilling 
on the land. 
Lompoc Oil retained the mineral rights to the 
remaining 200 acres, but has not yet developed them. 
Because Commissioner Stillman has an ownership interest in 
Lompoc Oil, valued at over $1,000, and, because he is a director 
of the company, he may not vote to approve or reject the 
proposed zoning and permit changes, if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the proposed changes would have a material 
financial effect on Lompoc Oil.

and (d).)

company the size of Lompoc Oil (which had $7,250 in gross income

the company by $1,000 or more.

§ 87103. Financial interest 
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of 
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect. distinguishable from its effect on the public gener-
ally. on: 
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($ 1 ,()(X)). 
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($I,()(X). 
(c) Any source of income. other than loans by a commercial lending 
institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public 
without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) 
or more in value provided to. received by or promised to the public offiCial 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee. employee, or holds any position. of management.

trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent

Sec. 35-319. Oil Drilling and Production plans 
Sec. 35-319.1. Purpose and Intent. 
The purpose of 011 Drilling and Production Plans is to provide fOr" dis-
cretionary review of the facilities for oil and gas drilling and/or production 
which may. because of scale or location of development, have a significant 
potential for impacts on natural resources. The intent of the requirements for 
Oil Drilling and Production Plans is to insure that impacts on natural resources 
from such activities are minimized to the maximum extent feasible. 
Sec. 35-319.2. Applicability of Oil Drilling and Production Plans. 
No Land Use Permit shall be issued for any activity related to oil and gas 
drilling or production, including grading, for any property subject to the pro-
visions of this section until an Oil Drilling and Production Plan has been approved 
as provided herein. Only property included within the boundaries of an approved 
Oil Drilling and Production Plan shall be entitled to a Land Use Permit for oil 
drilling and production operations. 
Sec. 35-319.3. Contents of Oil Drilling and Production Plans. 
As many copies of an Oil Drilling and Production Plan as may be required shall 
be submitted to the Resource Management Department. Unless otherwise specifically 
waived by the Director, the information to be submitted as part of an Oil Drilling 
and Production Plan shall consist of the following: 
1. A U.S.G.S. map (7.5 minute series topographic) or facsimile showing the project 
site(s); lease boundaries; and the routing of existing and proposed roads and 
pipelines to be used in conjunction with the project. 
2. A plot plan, drawn to scale, showing: 
a) Property lines;

Sec. 35-319.4. Processing of Oil Drilling and Production Plans. 
1. The applicant may apply for: the drilling operations only; production facil; es 
only; or both drilling and production facilities. 
2. After receipt of the Oil Brilling and Production Plan, the Resource Management 
Department shall process the plan through environmental review. 
3. The Resource Management Department shall refer the Oil Drilling Production Plan 
to appropriate County departments for review. 
4. The Planning Commission shall then consider the Oil Drilling and Production Pl 
at a noticed public hearing and approve, conditionally approve, or disapprove 
the plan. The Planning Commission's action shall be final subject to appeal to 
the Board of Supervisors as provided in Sec. 35-327.

Sec. 35-319.5. Findings Required for Approval of Oil Drilling and Production Plans. 
An Oil Drilling and Production Plan shall only be approved if all of 
following applicable findings are made: 
1. There are no feasi e alternative locations for the proposed project that are 
less environmentally damaging. 
2. Significant adverse environmental effects are mitigated to the mdximum extent 
feasible. 
3. The project will not be detrimental to the health, safety, and general welfare 
of the neighborhood and will not be incompatible with uses of the surrounding area. 
4. The development is in conformance with the applicable provisions of this Article 
and the Comprehensive Plan. 
5. When a permit for drilling only is requested, subsequent oil and gas production 
can be accommodated should the proposed drilling program be Successful. 
6. For projects requiring a Conditional Use Permit, the findings in Sec. 35-315.8 
(Conditional Use Permits) shall also apply.

or Planning Commission may impose appropriate and reasonable conditions as 
deemed necessary to protect persons or property, to preserve the natural resources 
or scenic quality of the area. to preserve the public health, safety, and 
welfare, or to i

AND FINAL REVISED DRAFT ON OIL AND GAS ZONING REGULATIONS 
I. 
INTRODUCTION 
Certain background information is helpful for an undex-
standing of the permit process. 
Three County Agencies grant various different permits 
concerning oil drilling and oil production. 
These are explained 
below. 
A. 
A2encies:

2.

such as Land Use Permit (LUP). 
3. 
Petroleum Office 
An office within the Public Works Department which 
grants both ~nisterial and Discretionary Permits. 
Its review 
is limited to safety and engineering considerations as opposed 
to above two agencies who consider land use matters.

II. 
EXISTING ZONING ORDINANCE 661 -
OIL DRILLING 
A. 
"0" Overlay: 
Provisions for oil and gas facilities are scattered through-
out the ordinance. 
There is not a specific section on oil and 
gas as is found in the proposed ordinance (Division 8). 
The 
main part of the existing ordinance which deals with oil and 
gas drilling is the "0" Oil Drilling Combining Regulations 
OVerlay Zone District, Art. VI, § 1, Ordinance 661. 
The "0" 
overlay may be applied to any zone district, i.e., residential, 
commercial, agriculture, etc., by rezone. 
This "0" overlay 
permits oil and gas drilling through a special oil well permit 
and in addition, oil and gas processing facilities by Conditional 
Use Permit. 
All uses under the "0" overlay require discretionary 
action from the Planning Commission. 
There are also specific 
permit conditions (See Sec. 1.2) and optional special conditions 
(Sec. 1.3) set forth in this "0" overlay. 


III. 
NEW ORDINANCE 
A. 
In the regulations as proposed in the February 1982 Draft, 
the ItO" overlay has been eliminated and a one step process 
was developed. 
Oil drilling would be permitted in many 
districts without the requirement of a rezone. 
A major differ-
ence between the existing ordinance and the first draft of 
the proposed Ordinance which was to change from ministerial 
to discretionary the nature of the drilling permit in certain 
agricultural zone districts in specific circumstances. 
Exploration (drilling outside State designated oil fields) 
and Production (drilling inside oil fields) operations were 
separated into two sections (Sec. 35-295, 35-296). 
This is 
intended to provide discretionary review when a project is 
located outside an established oil field in order to assure 
land use review prior to State expansion of oil fields. 
This 
would also coordinate permit procedures with the County 
Petroleum Office which requires discretionary petroleum permits 
outside designated oil fields and ministerial permits within 
oil fields.

Under the existing ordinance, oil drilling projects outside

In the first proposed draft (February 1982) there 
were six major sections (Exploratory Oil & Gas Drilling, 
Onshore Oil and Gas Production, Treatment & Processing Facili-
ties, Refining, Marine Terminals and Oil & Gas pipelines). 
Each 
of these sections contained specific development standards. 
Revisions to the original draft modified these standards. 
A 
point by point review may be made by comparing the development 
standards in the February draft to the August draft to deter-
mine the possible significance of these changes. 
Additionally, some of the development standards were changed 
from mandatory to optional at the discretion of the Planning 
Commission to impose standards. 
(See 35-295, #4., b.) 
IV. 
PETROLEUM OFFICE FUNCTION 
As indicated on all three charts, a Petroleum Permit is 
issued by the Petroleum Office for oil drilling operations. 
The Petroleum Office is co~tained within the Public Works 
Department. 
It reviews applications for drilling for consider-
ation of safety and engineering aspects only. 
Therefore, its 
function is only marginally relevant to this zoning ordinance 
since it does not review applications for land use consider-
ations. 
Land use matters are considered by the Resource Management 
Department through the Land Use Permits, Oil Well Permits, and 
other permits issued by this department.

SUMMARY OF PROPOSED OIL AND GAS ZONING REGULATIONS 
Introduction 
The existing zoning ordinance was adopted over thirty years ago. Over the 
years many concerns have been raised by decision makers and the public over the 
adequacy of this ordinance to appropriately regulate land use. In order to ad-
dress these concerns, update regulations, and implement the newly adopted Com-
prehensive Plan, it was necessary to rewrite the zoning ordinance, including 
regulations on oil and gas development. 
In doing so, a new format was developed 
to provide organizational consistency throughout the three County zoning ordinances. 
Listed below is a summary of the basis for the proposed oil and gas regulations. 
1. Comprehensive Plan Policies 
One of the main purposes in preparing the zoning ordinance is to implement 
the Comprehensive Plan. Since there are a number of policies within the Compre-
hensive Plan that are applicable to oil and gas development, it was necessary 
to incorporate provisions of these policies within the energy section. 
A. 
One of the more specific policies states, (Conservation Element) 
"No mineral resource extraction should be permitted in the County if 
significant adverse impacts on the air, water, or land environment 
would result, if flooding and erosion problems would be increased, 
or if polluting emissions likely to be generated directly or indirectly 
by the activity in question would result in adopted federal or State 
environmental quality standards being exceeded." 
B. 
There are several hillside and watershed protection policies that require 
protection of existing landforms through minimized grading. (Land Use Element) 
C. 
There are policies directly addressing preservation of archaeological sites. 
(Land Use Element) 
D. 
There are policies addressing the protection of visual resources within 
both rural and urban areas. (Land Use Element) 
E. 
The Noise Element of the Comprehensive Plan sets forth specific standards 
for permitted levels of noise and since the County does not have a noise 
ordinance, these standards must be implemented throughout the zoning ordinance. 
F. 
There are also policies contained within the various area/community goals 
section of the Land Use Element that express concern for review of oil and 
gas development. 
2. Addressing Problems 
The Petroleum Department and other County Departments have received numerous 
complaints relating to land use concerns, i.e., noise, odor, traffic impacts, etc., 
over the years, which are generated by oil and gas exploration and production. The 
proposed regulations set forth standards which will provide some protection from 
these impacts and require land use compatibility (i.e. soundproofing of equipment, 
minimum setbacks from residences, limitation on traffic through residential areas, etc.) 
A number of these standards are taken from the existing regulations on oil and gas 
development. 


3. Specificity 
In the existing regulations, a number of the facilities associated with oil and 
gas development have not been adequately addressed. thereby resulting in problems 
in the permit procedures regarding these projects. Examples are pipelines, treat-
ment and processing facilities, and marine terminals. The proposed regulations 
clearly address these types of facilities to assist both staff and the industry in 
processing projects. 
4. 
Fl ex; bil i ty 
A modification was made to provide additional flexihility to the oil industry 
in the manner of processing permits for exploratory wells. The existing ordinance 
results in a single permit being issued for both the exploration and production 
stages, even though most wells are dry holes and production does not take place. 
This often results in a lengthy environmental review which includes considerable 
speculation as to the required production facilities. To remedy this situation, ') 
the proposed regulations would give the industry the option to file for a permit 
~ 
covering the exploration phase initially, and then file for a permit covering pro-) 
duction if the drilling program is successful. In many cases, this flexibility 
would significantly shorten the processing ttme for exploratory wells. 
Comearison of Existing and Proposed Oil Regulations 
Since the proposed regulations are ;n a comp1etely new format, several charts 
are attached to compare existing regulations with the proposed provisions. There 
are several major changes which are outlined below. 
1. Elimination of the "0" Overlay 
Existing Zoning Ordinance No. 661 includes an overlay zone district entitled 
"0 - Oil and Gas Drilling Combining Regulations". This overlay district, which allows 
oil and gas exploration and production drilling, may be applied to any zone district. 
Since the "Oil was applied to property in most cases many years ago, its present appli-
cation is not appropriate in some areas and is suitable in other areas where no liD" 
overlay exists. Also, even though a property may be zoned "0", an oil well permit 
for oil drilling must first be obtained from the Planning Commission in a public 
hearing prior to drilling. Thus it is a two step process: 1) 
ReZoning property to 
IIOH; and 2} 
Obtaining a permit from the Planning Commission for each drilling project. 
The Planning Commission is considering the elimination of the "0" overlay and in its 
place, the use of a one step Conditional Use Permit procedure. This would streamline 
the process and yet continue to provide individual review for each project. Therefore, 
the new ordinance may allow oil and gas drilling by Conditional Use Permit (Planning 
Commission approval) in single family residential districts, and as a permitted use 
in the agricultural and general industrial zones. (See attached charts) 
2 


2. 
Exploration and Production Plans 
As proposed in the draft ordinance, the majority of oil wells which occur in 
agricultural zones within designateoOilf.ields will proceed with only a Land Use 
Permrras-the-i-ti-ave~ Tnthe~~past. ~However, each appl i cation for a land Use Permi t 
would be quickly checked against the criteria listed in the ordinance to determine 
if there is a substantial likelihood of significant environmental impacts such as 
disturbance of streams or archaeology sites, or usage of large quantities of ground 
water. The few projects which do not meet the listed criteria will require environ-
mental review and an approved production plan. 
Another change in the proposed ordinance from previous department procedures 
concerns the issuance of oil permits in agriculturEl_?_on_es.o~utside of-rlesi9n~t~d 
oil f;elds~~ Under the current regulations, environmental review is conducted by 
the Resource Management Department and a permit is issued by the Petroleum Office. 
Under the proposed ordinance, ~Qe~£]oration plan would be approved by the Resource 
Management Department pri or to the i SSlJance of permit by the Petroleum Offi ce. Thi s 
procedure would allow for the evaluation of land use issues when drilling is proposed 
in areas previously free of oil development. Additionally, this would provide for 
land use review prior to expansion of oil fields.

The facts, as I understand them, are that Santa Barbara 
County is currently reviewing and revising its zoning 
ordinance. 
This review includes that portion of the zoning 
ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities 
(Divisions 8 and 10). 
The County's Resource Management 
Department began the review process by preparing a draft of 
Divisons 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. 
This 
draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were 
conducted. 
As a result of these hearings, and after discussions 
by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of 
Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing 
textual changes requested by the Commission. 
The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to 
approve the Revised Hearing Draft. 
If passed, the Draft will be 
submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, 
modified or disapproved. 
(See Government Code Sections 
65854-65857)!/ 
However, the recommendations of the Planning 
!/ Hereinafter John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
Commission will greatly influence the Board of Supervisors in 
its final decision regarding the oil and gas regulations. 
The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter 
the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit 
procedures. 
These changes include: 
1. 
Banning oil development in seven areas where 
development was previously allowed; 
2. 
Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas 
exploration and production drilling by eliminating a 
requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a 
permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use 
Permit be obtained; 
3. 
Establishing more stringent "development 
standards"~1 that must be met for exploratory oil and gas 
drilling; onshore oil and gas production; treatment and 
processing facilities; refining; marine terminals; and oil 
and gas pipelines; 
4. 
Establishing a procedure whereby a ministerially 
granted exploration and production permit may be obtained if 
a project meets certain, established criteria,ll but if 
the criteria are not met, a discretionary permit must be 
sought from the Planning Commission. 
This new procedure 
provides the Planning Commission with more discretion'than 
under the existing system.il 
~I For example, in the Revised Hearing Draft, the 
"development standards" for treatment and processing facilities 
require noise and noxious odor limitations, a fence surrounding 
the facility and provisions to prevent erosion and flood damage. 
11 
These criteria establish that the project will not 
have a significant environmental impact. 
il Under the existing system, a dicretionary, rather 
than ministerial, permit must be obtained if drilling will be 
within 1,000 feet of a zone on which drilling is not allowed. 
The new system requires a discretionary permit if drilling is 
within this 1,000 foot setback, as well as in many other 
situations, such as where the company plans to build a treatment 
or processing facility. 


John M. Cohan 
November 5, 1982  
5. 
Allowing oil companies additional flexibility in 
obtaining permits for exploratory wells. 
Currently, one 
permit is issued for both the exploration and production 
stages of oil and gas development. 
In the case of 
unproductive wells,21 this results in unnecessary, lengthy 
environmental review of the production phase. 
The proposed 
procedures allow oil companies to first file for an 
exploration permit and later file for a production permit, 
if the well is successful. 
6. 
Replacing existing, vague provisions for oil and 
gas pipelines with provisions requiring the Planning 
Commission to issue discretionary permits. 
"Development 
standards" are also established for pipelines (see #3, 
above) • 
Your question is whether Commissioners Hamister, Lahr and 
Stillman may vote on the Revised Hearing Draft, or whether they 
have conflicts of interests requiring them to abstain.

Planning Commissioner Hamister currently owns common stock 
in Exxon Corporation, valued at between $10,001 and $100,000. 
Section 87100 provides that no local government official shall 
make, or participate in the making of, a governmental decision 
in which she knows, or has reason to know, she has a financial 
interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on any business entity in which the public official has a 
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. 
(Section 
87103(a) ). 
Thus, Commissioner Hamister must abstain from making

Under Commission regulations, a decision

gross income by $100,000, its annual net income by $50,000, or 
the value of its assets or liabilities by $100,000. 
(2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (1) (A)-(C).) 
As I discussed in my advice letter to Mr. Cohan, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to amend the oil and

or participating in the making of, this decision.

Commissioner Hamister may have already participated in 
discussions or decisions occurring prior to this final vote.

my letter, I did not discuss the propriety of her participation 
and any enforcement action would, of course, focus on these past 
activities.

This review includes that portion of the zoning ordinance regulating land use for oil and gas facilities (Divisions 8 and 10). The County's Resource Management Department began the review process by preparing a draft of Divisons 8 and 10, containing the staff's recommendations. This draft was presented to the Planning Commission and hearings were conducted. As a result of these hearings, and after discussions by the Planning Commission, a final Revised Hearing Draft of Divisions 8 and 10 (dated 8/19/82) was prepared, containing textual changes requested by the Commission. The Planning Commission will vote on whether or not to approve the Revised Hearing Draft. If passed, the Draft will be submitted to the Board of Supervisors where it may be adopted, modified or disapproved. The changes proposed by the Planning Commission would alter the existing oil and gas zoning provisions and permit procedures. These changes include: 1. Banning oil development in seven areas where development was previously allowed; 2. Streamlining the permit procedure for oil and gas exploration and production drilling by eliminating a requirement that oil companies obtain both a rezoning and a permit, and requiring, instead, that only a Conditional Use Permit be obtained; 3.
